content: Placebo response measurement in trials: difference between baseline and endpoint in placebo arm (placebo response rate), proportion of placebo patients meeting response criteria (e.g., ≥50% pain reduction, ≥30% HAM-D decrease in depression). High placebo rates can obscure drug-placebo differences, leading to trial failure despite true drug efficacy. Factors increasing placebo response include: subjective endpoints, frequent site visits, elaborate rituals, patient expectations, milder disease severity, and shorter trial duration. Nocebo adverse event rates in placebo arms often reach 20-30%, complicating safety signal detection. Ethical considerations limit placebo use when effective treatments exist; non-inferiority designs may be required. Meta-analyses show increasing placebo responses over time in some indications (e.g., neuropathic pain, depression), potentially due to trial design changes and patient expectations.
